content.text: What Makes a Design Review Different for Claude Experiences

A conventional software design review checks whether the proposed architecture satisfies the functional requirements and meets non-functional constraints. A Claude experience design review does all of that and must additionally verify something conventional software never needs to: that the intended behaviour is explicitly specified, that there is an automated way to measure whether the model produces that behaviour, and that the known failure modes have been catalogued and mitigated.

You cannot review a Claude feature by reading the code alone. The model's behaviour at runtime is a function of the system prompt, the conversation context, and non-deterministic sampling — none of which appear in the source tree. The review must inspect the behaviour spec, the eval plan, and the prototype evidence.
This sub-subtopic provides a structured checklist across six dimensions, a scoring rubric, an automated review runner, and guidance on what each item is actually checking and why it matters.

attrs.alt: Six design review dimensions: Behaviour Spec Coverage, Evaluation Plan, Risk & Ethics, Prototype Evidence, Prompt Design Quality, Operational Readiness

content.text: Each dimension is reviewed by a specific owner. The review is not complete until all six dimensions reach GREEN status. A single FAIL blocks sign-off regardless of how well other dimensions score.
The behaviour spec is the contract between product and engineering. The review checks that the contract is complete enough to be tested.
No requirement should exist without a corresponding must-do item in the spec
A must-do with no test_signal cannot be evaluated — it is aspirational, not verifiable
Must-not-do list covers top failure modes
The FMEA should drive must-not-do items; any P0/P1 failure mode not reflected is a gap
If the feature uses tool-use or structured output, the schema must be in the spec
Spec version hash tracked in registry
Without drift detection, prompt edits silently invalidate the spec
The eval plan review checks that the team has a concrete, automated way to measure model behaviour against the spec. Vague statements like 'we will test this manually' are not acceptable at sign-off.
Eval suite exists for every must-do item
Manual review of 5 examples is not an eval suite; the reviewer must see an automated runner
Thresholds should be calibrated from prototype testing, not set to 100%
A suite of only happy-path inputs is not a suite; boundary and negative cases are required
If running the eval requires a human, it will not run at the frequency needed
Every past production failure should contribute one eval case
Risk and ethics review is the hardest to automate and the most often skipped. It requires human judgement on questions that cannot be fully specified in advance.
FMEA run; top 5 failure modes mitigated
An FMEA is a structured exercise, not a brainstorm. The RPN scores must be present.
Any feature where the model makes factual claims needs an accuracy gate in the eval plan
Conversation logs may contain PII; data retention policy must be declared
Eval inputs should include representative samples of the target user population
Any feature touching regulated domains (health, finance, legal) requires legal review
The prototype evidence review confirms that the design was tested with real users before engineering begins. Without it, the team is building based on assumptions about user behaviour.
All four script types must be present, not just happy path
Internal team members do not count; the participants must be representative users
Testing that produces no revisions is not being read seriously
The handoff package must link prototype → eval → ticket for each requirement
A matrix only in someone's inbox does not exist for the purposes of review

The prompt design review is the most technical of the six dimensions. It checks whether the system prompt is derived from the behaviour spec, not written independently, and that no manual edits have been made since the last eval run.

The operational readiness review ensures the team can detect problems in production and respond to them. A Claude experience that ships without observability hooks is effectively invisible until users complain.
Latency, error rate, and refusal rate metrics must exist before launch
If the model behaves unexpectedly in production, the team needs a documented way to revert
Uncapped API usage can create unexpected cost spikes; caps must be set
The runbook must tell on-call engineers what metric values indicate a model regression
Full launch without canary means all users experience any regression simultaneously
Run all six dimensions in parallel and produce a consolidated sign-off report.
CI Gate: Block Merge Without Sign-Off

# .github/workflows/design-sign-off-gate.yml
name: Design Sign-Off Gate

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'src/prompts/**'
      - 'src/content/subjects/**'
      - 'evals/**'

jobs:
  sign-off-check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Check design sign-off record
        run: |
          # Each AI feature must have a sign-off record in design-reviews/
          FEATURE=$(python3 scripts/detect_ai_feature_change.py)
          REVIEW_FILE="design-reviews/${FEATURE}.json"
          if [ ! -f "$REVIEW_FILE" ]; then
            echo "FAIL: No design review record for feature: $FEATURE"
            echo "Run: npm run design:review -- --feature $FEATURE"
            exit 1
          fi
          STATUS=$(jq -r .sign_off_status "$REVIEW_FILE")
          if [ "$STATUS" != "SIGNED_OFF" ]; then
            echo "FAIL: Design review status is $STATUS for $FEATURE"
            jq .blocking_issues "$REVIEW_FILE"
            exit 1
          fi
          echo "PASS: Design review SIGNED_OFF for $FEATURE"
: design review must happen before engineering starts, not after. A post-build review is an audit, not a gate.
: the features that cause the most harm in production are those the team considered too simple to warrant a full risk review.
: 'the team tried it and it seemed fine' is not prototype evidence. The review must see the actual session notes and synthesis.
: a YELLOW dimension means a condition must be met before the feature ships. It does not mean sign-off is granted.
: each dimension has a designated owner because no single person has deep expertise in all six areas. A solo review is incomplete by construction.

A design review for a Claude experience checks six dimensions: behaviour spec coverage (is the contract complete and testable?), evaluation plan (is there an automated way to measure compliance?), risk and ethics (have failure modes been catalogued and mitigated?), prototype evidence (was the design tested with real users?), prompt design quality (was the prompt derived from the spec and not silently edited?), and operational readiness (can the team detect and respond to production problems?). All six must reach GREEN for sign-off to be granted. Any RED dimension blocks the feature from moving to build, regardless of schedule pressure. Automating the review with structured tool calls and a CI gate makes the process repeatable and audit-friendly.
